speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub, episode 36: Organizations and Templates. I am Alex. Today we are going to make Organizations and Templates something you can explain, practice, and recover from when the interface surprises you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will be the voice of the learner who is willing to ask, what is this for, where am I, and how do I know I did it right?

speaker: ALEX
text: The big idea today: GitHub Organizations, repository templates, visibility, and archiving. We will name the concept, explain why it matters, practice the move, and point out the checks that tell you the work is real.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the episode should work even if someone has not read the chapter yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The transcript has to stand on its own. It can point toward practice, but it should teach the concept right here in the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Building Your Community Presence on GitHub: This appendix covers three community-facing GitHub features: your profile README (how the world sees you), GitHub Sponsors (financially supporting the people whose work you depend on), and GitHub Wikis (community-editable documentation inside a repository).

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: Profiles, Sponsors, and Wikis has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. Your profile README is announced as regular page content when someone visits your profile -- use headings for structure so visitors can navigate with H. The Sponsor button on a profile or repo is a standard button element -- press B to cycle through buttons until you hear "Sponsor". Wiki pages are read in browse mode like any other GitHub Markdown page -- use heading navigation to jump between sections. Profile READMEs respect GitHub's dark and light themes -- test yours in both modes to confirm text remains readable. The Sponsor button uses a heart icon with a pink/magenta accent -- look near the repo name or profile photo area. Wiki sidebar navigation appears on the right side of the page with links to all wiki pages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside The Special Profile README: GitHub has a hidden feature: if you create a repository named exactly your-username/your-username (e.g., janesmith/janesmith), the README in that repo appears on your GitHub profile page. That matters in practice: It's a custom introduction visible to anyone who visits your profile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating Your Profile README has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Create a new repository; Name it exactly your-username (match your GitHub username exactly, case-sensitive); Make it public; and Initialize with a README. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is edit the README with whatever you want to show on your profile. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: What to include has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Introduction: Who you are, what you work on. Current focus: What projects or technologies you're learning. Skills: Languages, frameworks, tools (optional). How to reach you: Email, LinkedIn, personal site. Fun facts: Hobbies, interests (optional-keeps it human).

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Example profile README has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Hi, I'm Jane Smith; I'm an accessibility advocate and open source contributor focused on making the web more inclusive.; Current focus; - Contributing to NVDA documentation; - Building accessible React components; - Learning TypeScript; Skills; - JavaScript,. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Profile README Best Practices is still useful because keep it concise - visitors skim, not read Update occasionally - a README from 2019 looks stale Be authentic - people connect with real humans, not buzzwords Include links - make it easy to learn more or get in touch. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Avoid excessive badges - 50 skill badges is visual clutter and screen reader noise Skip auto-generated stats - "commits per day" widgets are often inaccessible Don't overthink it - a simple paragraph is better than nothing.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Screen Reader Considerations has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Use headings ( ) for structure. Provide alt text for any images. Avoid ASCII art - screen readers read it character by character (annoying). Test your README with a screen reader before publishing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pinned repositories (up to 6) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Highlight your best work on your profile. Navigate to your profile → Select "Customize your pins". Choose which repos appear first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. Contribution graph has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Shows your GitHub activity over the past year. Green squares indicate days with commits, PRs, issues, etc. Cannot be customized but reflects consistent contribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Status has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Set a temporary status message (e.g., "On vacation until March 15"). Navigate to your profile → Select the smile icon → Set status.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. GitHub Sponsors lets you financially support developers and projects you depend on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How it works. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How it works has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Developers/projects create a Sponsors profile. You choose a monthly sponsorship tier ($5, $10, $25/month, etc.). Your payment goes directly to the developer (GitHub takes no fees).

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Why Sponsor? has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Sustainability: Many open source maintainers volunteer their time. Sponsorships help them keep projects alive. Gratitude: If a project saved you hours of work, sponsorship is a way to say thanks. Priority support: Some maintainers offer sponsor-only Discord access, early releases, or prioritized bug fixes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: How to Sponsor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to a user or repository's GitHub page. Step two is look for the "Sponsor" button (heart icon). Step three is choose a tier or custom amount. Step four is select payment method (credit card or PayPal). The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, GitHub sends a receipt; your sponsorship appears on your profile (optionally publicly). Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Screen reader navigation has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. The Sponsor button appears near the profile photo or repo name. Press B to cycle through buttons on the page until you hear "Sponsor".

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Can I Receive Sponsorships? is still useful because if you maintain an open source project or contribute regularly. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Many accessibility advocates successfully use Sponsors to fund their work improving assistive technology and inclusive design.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate to github.com/sponsors. Then: Select "Join the waitlist" or "Set up sponsors". Next: Connect a payment method (Stripe or bank account). Last: Create sponsor tiers with descriptions. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Can I Receive Sponsorships? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Promote your Sponsors page to your audience. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put What Is a GitHub Wiki? into plain language. Every repository can have a wiki - a space for documentation separate from the code. The useful version is: It's lightweight and Markdown-based. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When to use a wiki. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: When to use a wiki has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Multi-page documentation (tutorials, guides, FAQs). Community-editable docs (wikis can be editable by anyone). Knowledge that doesn't belong in README (too long, too specific).

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. When NOT to use a wiki has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Your project already uses GitHub Pages or external docs. Documentation needs to be version-controlled with code (wikis are separate Git repos). You want full control (wikis are less customizable than Pages).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Accessing a Repo's Wiki. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Accessing a Repo's Wiki has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigate to the repository. Then, select the "Wiki" tab. After that, if no wiki exists, you'll see "Create the first page". That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating Wiki Pages. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. Wiki pages automatically appear in a sidebar for navigation. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to the Wiki tab. Then: Select "New page". Next: Add a title and content (Markdown). Last: Select "Save". That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Wiki Accessibility. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Wiki Accessibility: return to: Resources Appendix S - Organizations and Templates Appendix G - GitHub Discussions Appendix T - Contributing to Open Source Appendix A - Glossary.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. GitHub's wiki editor is the same as the issue/PR comment editor. All Markdown features work (headings, lists, links, code blocks). Use proper heading hierarchy (, ) for screen reader navigation. Link between wiki pages: [[Page Title]]. Screen reader caveat: Wiki pages are a separate Git repository. Any changes pushed directly to the wiki's git remote are not tracked by the main repository's branch protection - meaning no PR review process applies. Treat wikis as community-editable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Learning Cards: Organizations and Templates has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. The "Use this template" button is near the "Code" button at the top of a template repository -- press B to navigate buttons until you hear it. Organization profiles list repositories and members under heading sections -- use H to jump between People, Repositories, and Projects. Repository visibility (Public, Private, Internal) is announced as a badge near the repository name heading. Template repositories show a green "Use this template" button prominently near the top -- it replaces or sits alongside the Fork button. Organization pages use the same layout as personal profiles but with team-oriented sections. Archived repositories display a yellow warning banner across the top of the page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should someone choose between those options?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: these are two very different actions that both appear near the "Code" button. The listener should be able to check this: GitHub Skills courses use "Use this template" - you start fresh with the course scaffold but your copy has no upstream connection.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: Use when. means What you get. Contributing back to the original project means Full git history; your changes can be PRed upstream. Use this template means Starting a new project based on the structure means Clean git history; no connection to the original repo.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating a Template Repository. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Creating a Template Repository as decoration. Maintainers can mark any repository as a template. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigate to the repository's Settings tab. Then, scroll to the "General" section → find the "Template repository" checkbox. After that, check it and save. Finally, the repository now shows a "Use this template" button instead of (or alongside) "Fork". The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Using a Template Repository. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Using a Template Repository has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate to the template repository. Then: Select "Use this template" button (near the top, next to "Code"). Next: Select "Create a new repository". Last: Name your new repository, choose visibility, and confirm. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Using a Template Repository, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: GitHub creates a new repository with the template's files but no commit history. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Screen reader path has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like B → navigate buttons → find "Use this template" → Enter; ↓ → "Create a new repository" from the dropdown → Enter. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What Is an Organization? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. A GitHub organization is an account that multiple people share. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Instead of github.com/username/repo, organization repos live at github.com/org-name/repo.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Joining an Organization. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because maintainers can invite you to join. That gives the learner a foothold: you can also be a public contributor to an org repo without being a member - you fork the repo and submit PRs without needing an invitation. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is you receive an email + GitHub notification. Step two is navigate to github.com/settings/organizations to accept. Step three is or click the link in the invitation email. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Organization Membership Visibility. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Organization Membership Visibility has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. By default, your org membership is private (only you and org owners can see it). You can set it to public in your organization membership settings. Public membership appears on your GitHub profile under "Organizations". For community-access: if you become a member, set your membership public to show your contribution publicly on your profile.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Here is the learner-facing version. Organizations can create teams (e.g., @community-access/accessibility-reviewers). Put another way, when you see a team mentioned in a PR or issue, that @mention notifies everyone on that team.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Navigating an Organization Profile Page. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Navigating an Organization Profile Page: organization-level Projects (like the community-access project board) appear in the org's Projects tab, not inside any single repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on Repository Settings - What Contributors Need to Know. You may not have Settings access to most repositories (that requires maintainer role). This is the part to say slowly: But knowing what's there helps you understand why a repository behaves the way it does. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Repository Visibility. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: the repository's visibility label appears on its page. The listener should be able to check this: Screen readers: the visibility badge is usually near the repo name heading (H1).

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Do not treat Archived Repositories as decoration. When a maintainer archives a repository, it becomes read-only. That is not trivia. If you find a repo you planned to contribute to is archived, look for a fork or successor project.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. No new issues, PRs, or comments can be created. Existing content is fully preserved and viewable. The UI shows a yellow banner: "This repository has been archived by the owner.". Screen readers: NVDA/JAWS will read this banner when you navigate to the top of the page with Ctrl+Home.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Repository Topics. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Repository Topics is still useful because topics are keyword tags on a repository (e.g., accessibility, screen-reader, open-source). For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: They appear as colored chips on the repository home page and improve discoverability in GitHub search.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. As a contributor: Topics tell you what the project is about at a glance. As a maintainer: Add topics in Settings → General → Topics section to improve search ranking. Screen reader: Topics are links in the "About" sidebar section; use B (next button) or Links list to reach them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Put Default Branch Name into plain language. The default branch is the one all PRs target by default. The useful version is: Modern projects use main; older projects may use master or another name. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub Is More Than a Code Host -- It's a Community. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Who this is for: You have learned the basics of GitHub and want to know how to use it as a social platform -- discovering interesting projects, following developers whose work you admire, and building a presence in the open source community. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: This appendix covers the social layer of GitHub that most tutorials skip entirely.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Learning Cards: Social Features has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The Star button is announced as "Star this repository" or "Unstar this repository" -- press B to find it in the repository header area. The Follow button on a user profile is announced as "Follow [username]" -- Tab forward from the avatar and bio to reach it. On the Explore and Trending pages, each repository entry is a heading with a link -- use H or 3 to jump between entries. Star and Watch buttons sit side by side in the repository header with distinct icons (star and eye) -- both show counts next to them. Your contribution graph uses green intensity to show activity levels -- enable high-contrast theme if the color differences are hard to distinguish. The Trending page lists repositories in a numbered vertical list with star counts and daily gain on the right side.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What a star is. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What a star is: A star is GitHub's version of a bookmark combined with a "like." When you star a repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. It saves to your starred list at github.com/username?tab=stars -- easy to find later. It signals to the maintainer that their work is valued. It contributes to the project's star count, which helps others discover it. It may appear in your followers' feeds.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Here is the learner-facing version. Star counts are a social proof signal -- developers browsing for tools often sort by stars to find well-regarded projects. Put another way, a project going from 10 stars to 1,000 stars can dramatically change how many contributors it attracts. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub.com. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside GitHub.com: on any repository page, the Star button is in the top-right area of the repository header, next to Fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Click Star to star the repository -- the button changes to Starred with a filled star icon. Click the dropdown arrow next to Star to choose a List to organize it into (see Section 8). Click Starred to unstar.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Anchor this part on Keyboard shortcut. On a repository page, press g then s to toggle the star.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Screen reader navigation. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Screen reader navigation has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. NVDA/JAWS: The Star button is in the page region after the repository title heading. Navigate by button (B) or Tab to find it. It's announced as "Star this repository" or "Unstar this repository.". VoiceOver: VO+Command+J to jump to buttons, or Tab through the header area. The button label changes between "Star" and "Starred.".

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub CLI has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Star a repository; gh api user/starred/owner/repo --method PUT; Unstar a repository; gh api user/starred/owner/repo --method DELETE; List your starred repositories; gh api user/starred --jq '.[].full name'; Check if you've starred a repo; gh api. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Viewing your stars. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Viewing your stars is still useful because go to github.com/username?tab=stars -- or click your avatar → Your stars. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: You'll see all your starred repositories sorted by most recently starred.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. Learning Cards: Stars has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The Star button is in the repository header area -- press B to navigate buttons until you hear "Star this repository" (or "Unstar this repository" if already starred); press Enter to toggle. The keyboard shortcut g then s on any repository page toggles the star without needing to navigate to the button. Your starred repos are listed at github.com/username?tab=stars -- each entry is a heading with the repo name as a link; use H to jump between starred repos. The Star button shows a star icon with a count next to it in the repository header -- at high zoom, the button may wrap below the repo name; look for the star icon near Fork and Watch. When you star a repo, the button changes from an outline star to a filled star with the label "Starred" -- the visual change is subtle; confirm by re-reading the button text. The dropdown arrow next to Star lets you add the repo to a List -- look for a small triangle icon to the right of the Star button at high magnification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What watching does. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. When you watch a repository, GitHub sends you notifications about activity in it -- new issues, pull requests, releases, and more. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Unlike stars (which are passive bookmarks), watching is active -- you're opting into the conversation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because GitHub gives you granular control over how much you hear from a repo. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to watch a repository. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to watch a repository: On any repository page, the Watch button is next to the Star button in the header. The next useful detail is concrete: For most repositories you contribute to, "Participating and @mentions" is the right level -- you hear about threads you're in without inbox overload.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, click Watch to open the dropdown. Then, choose your notification level. After that, the button updates to show your current setting. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Watch a repository (all activity); gh api repos/owner/repo/subscription --method PUT --field subscribed=true; Watch releases only (requires GitHub.com -- not available via API alone); Use the web UI for granular watch levels; Ignore a repository; gh api. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Watching Repositories. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Watching Repositories has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The Watch button is next to the Star button in the repository header -- press B to navigate buttons until you hear "Watch" or a watch level label; press Enter to open the dropdown. The watch level dropdown presents radio-style options (Not watching, Participating, All Activity, Releases only, Ignore) -- arrow through them and press Enter to select. "Participating and @mentions" is the recommended default for repos you contribute to -- it notifies you only for threads you are in or mentioned in, avoiding inbox overload. The Watch button shows an eye icon with a dropdown arrow and a count of watchers -- at high zoom, look for it immediately to the left of the Star button in the repository header. The dropdown menu lists five watch levels vertically with radio indicators -- the currently selected level has a filled radio dot or checkmark next to it. "Releases only" is the best choice for tools and dependencies you use but do not contribute to -- it sends one notification per release instead of every issue and PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on What following does. When you follow a developer on GitHub. This is the part to say slowly: Following is one-way (like Twitter/X) -- they don't need to follow you back. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Their public activity appears in your home feed. You see when they star a repository, create a new repo, or get a new follower. They receive a notification that you followed them. You appear in their followers list.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Who to follow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: start with people whose work you already use.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Maintainers of tools and libraries you use daily. Authors of blog posts or talks that helped you learn. Developers in accessibility, open source, or your tech stack. Visiting the Contributors tab of a repository you love: github.com/owner/repo/graphs/contributors. Checking who opened issues or PRs you found valuable. Looking at who your existing follows follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat How to follow someone as decoration. On any user profile page (github.com/username), click the Follow button below their avatar. That is not trivia. To unfollow: click Following → it changes back to Follow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Screen reader navigation, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Navigate to the profile page. The Follow/Following button is near the top of the page, below the avatar and bio. NVDA/JAWS: press B to jump to buttons; the button is labelled "Follow [username]". VoiceOver: Tab to the button or use VO+Command+J.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub CLI. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Follow a user; gh api user/following/username --method PUT; Unfollow a user; gh api user/following/username --method DELETE; List who you're following; gh api user/following --jq '.[].login'; List your followers; gh api user/followers --jq '.[].login'; Check. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Viewing someone's profile. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The contribution graph is a visual calendar that screen readers may announce as a table or grid. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Navigate with arrow keys to read individual day entries -- each cell describes the date and number of contributions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Pinned repositories -- the 6 repos they've chosen to highlight. Contribution graph -- a visual grid of their activity over the past year (green squares = more activity). Recent activity -- PRs opened, issues commented on, repos starred. Repositories -- all their public repos.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because when you go to github.com while logged in, your home feed shows activity from people and repositories you follow or watch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What appears in your feed. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What appears in your feed has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Repositories starred by people you follow -- "Jane starred awesome-accessibility". New repositories created by people you follow. Releases from repositories you watch. Public activity from people you follow (PRs opened, issues commented on). "For you" recommendations -- GitHub suggests repos and people based on your activity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. One of the best ways to find new interesting projects is to follow a few active developers in your area of interest and watch what they star. Put another way, if 5 people you respect all starred the same new tool this week, it's probably worth a look. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Customising your feed. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Customising your feed: there's no fine-grained feed filter -- you control the feed by controlling who you follow and what you watch. That matters in practice: Unfollow noisy accounts, follow more focused ones.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on 5. GitHub Explore -- Discovering New Projects. GitHub Explore at github.com/explore is the discovery hub -- curated collections, trending repos, and personalised recommendations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What Explore shows. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What Explore shows has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Trending -- most-starred repos this week (see Section 6). Topics -- browse by subject area (see Section 7). Collections -- curated lists of thematically related repos (e.g., "Tools for Open Source", "Accessibility Projects"). "For you" personalised recommendations -- based on your stars, follows, and language preferences.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Navigating Explore with a screen reader. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Navigating Explore with a screen reader has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, go to github.com/explore. Then, the page uses landmark regions -- jump to main to skip navigation. After that, collections and trending repos are listed as article/heading groups. Finally, use heading navigation (H) to jump between sections. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Navigating Explore with a screen reader, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Each repo entry has a heading (repo name as a link), language badge, star count, and description. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 6. Trending -- What's Popular Right Now. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 6. Trending -- What's Popular Right Now is still useful because GitHub Trending at github.com/trending shows repositories gaining the most stars over a time period. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It's one of the best places to discover new tools before everyone else knows about them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Filtering trending into plain language. Use the dropdowns at the top of the page to filter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Trending developers. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Switch to github.com/trending/developers to see which developers are gaining the most followers -- another great way to find people to follow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave GitHub CLI, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Trending repos aren't in the official API, but you can get recently starred popular repos:; gh search repos --sort stars --order desc --limit 20 --language markdown; Trending in a specific language; gh search repos --sort stars --order desc --limit 20. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 7. Topics -- Finding Projects by Category. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 7. Topics -- Finding Projects by Category: Every repository can be tagged with topics -- keywords like accessibility, screen-reader, wcag, python, machine-learning. The next useful detail is concrete: Topics are how maintainers categorise their work so others can discover it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Click any topic tag on a repository page to see all repos tagged with that topic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the teaching move inside GitHub CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Search for repos with a specific topic; gh search repos --topic accessibility --limit 20; gh search repos --topic screen-reader --stars " 50"; Add a topic to your own repository; gh api repos/owner/repo/topics --method PUT --field names[]="accessibility". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on 8. GitHub Lists -- Organizing Your Stars. Lists let you group your starred repositories into named collections -- like playlists for code. This is the part to say slowly: Instead of one big pile of stars, you can have "Accessibility Tools," "Learning Resources," "Projects I Contribute To," etc. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating a list. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating a list has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is go to your stars: github.com/username?tab=stars. Step two is select "Create list" (top right of the stars page). Step three is give it a name and optional description. Step four is select "Create". Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Creating a list, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, on a repo page, click the dropdown arrow next to the Star button. Then, select "Create a list" or add to an existing list. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Adding repos to lists has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. From any repo page: Star dropdown → check the list name. From your stars page: click the list icon on any starred repo row.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Viewing and sharing lists. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Viewing and sharing lists is still useful because your lists are public at github.com/username?tab=stars -- anyone can browse them. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: This is useful for sharing curated resources with your community.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If someone only remembers one thing from GitHub CLI, what should it be?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Lists are managed through the GitHub web interface only; You can view stars via CLI:; gh api user/starred --jq '.[] {name.full name, description.description}' head -20. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 9. Finding Accessible and Inclusive Projects. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. If you're specifically looking for projects that welcome contributors with disabilities, or that focus on accessibility work, here are the best ways to find them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Search strategies. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Search strategies has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. github.com/topics/accessibility. github.com/topics/wcag. github.com/topics/screen-reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Search for accessibility-focused repos; gh search repos "accessibility" --topic a11y --stars " 100"; Find repos with good first issues in accessibility; gh search issues "accessibility" --label "good first issue" --state open; Find issues tagged both. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Organisations to follow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Organisations to follow: GitHub organisations are collections of repos grouped by a team or company. The next useful detail is concrete: You can follow an org to get notified of their public activity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. github.com/Community-Access -- the organisation behind this workshop. Search for org:github accessibility to find GitHub's own accessibility work. Many assistive technology companies have open source components on GitHub -- search for your AT provider.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. If you follow someone doing accessibility work, browse their stars and their following list -- this is one of the fastest ways to discover the accessibility community on GitHub. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 10. Building Your Own Presence. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 10. Building Your Own Presence: being visible on GitHub matters when you want to collaborate with others, get hired, or establish yourself as a contributor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Your contribution graph. The green grid on your profile shows your public contribution activity over the past year. This is the part to say slowly: Contributions to private repos only appear as grey squares unless the repo is made public later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Push commits to a public repo. Open, comment on, or close issues or PRs in a public repo. Review a PR in a public repo.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Pinning repositories. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: pin up to 6 repositories (your own or repos you've contributed to) on your profile.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is go to your profile (github.com/username). Step two is select "Customize your pins" above the pinned repos section. Step three is check up to 6 repos to pin -- prioritise your best work and most active contributions. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Your profile README as decoration. Create a special repository named exactly the same as your username (github.com/username/username) and its README.md will appear at the top of your profile page. That is not trivia. This is your chance to introduce yourself, list your skills, and share what you're working on. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Activity tips. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Activity tips has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Comment thoughtfully on issues -- even "I can reproduce this on Windows 11 with NVDA" is a valued contribution that shows on your profile. Star generously -- it signals your interests and others see it in their feeds. Follow people in your area -- they often follow back, growing your network organically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 11. The GitHub CLI for Social Features. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 11. The GitHub CLI for Social Features has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like --- Following ---; gh api user/following/username --method PUT Follow someone; gh api user/following/username --method DELETE Unfollow; gh api user/following --jq '.[].login' List who you follow; gh api user/followers --jq '.[].login' List your followers; ---. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Following a user. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Following a user has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Navigate to github.com/username; Press H to jump through headings to find the user's name at the top; Tab forward -- the Follow/Following button is within the first few interactive elements after the avatar/bio area; and Press Enter or Space to follow; the button label updates to "Following [username]". Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Starring a repository. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Starring a repository has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to any repository page. Step two is the Star button is in the repository header, near the Fork button. Step three is press B (NVDA/JAWS) to navigate by button, or Tab through the header. Step four is the button is announced as "Star this repository" or "Unstar this repository". The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Starring a repository, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, after starring, the button label changes and a count updates. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Browsing your stars. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Browsing your stars has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, go to github.com/username?tab=stars. Then, jump to main landmark to skip navigation. After that, each starred repo is a heading (H3) with a link -- navigate with H or 3. Finally, below each heading: description text, language, star count, and list controls. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Exploring topics. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exploring topics has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to github.com/topics/accessibility (or any topic). Then: Jump to main landmark. Next: Repos are listed as article regions with H3 headings. Last: Each entry has: repo name (link), owner, description, language, star count, and a Star button. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub Explore and Trending. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside GitHub Explore and Trending: next: Appendix U: Discussions and Gists Back: Appendix S: Releases and Insights Teaching chapter: Chapter 08: Open Source Culture.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Go to github.com/explore or github.com/trending; Use H to navigate between sections and repo entries; Trending page has language and time period filter dropdowns near the top -- Tab to find them; and Each trending repo row has: rank position, repo name (link), description, star count, and "Stars today" count. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 36. Next in the series is episode 37, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
